> **⚑ Measured update (Milestone 1, 2026-06-01):** several estimates below are now measured — see
> [MILESTONE_1_RESULTS.md](MILESTONE_1_RESULTS.md). Key deltas: the **protocol/net surface is now
> *proven*** (48/48 constants byte-faithful; primitives byte-identical to oracles and to the jar by
> reflection; empty net decompile-diff), so the fidelity risk that loaded Options A/C is materially
> lower *for net*. The deob's compile blocker turned out to be a **mechanical visibility/packaging
> split, not obfuscation drift** (~7 genuine drift sites), which lowers the cost of the "make it
> compile" prerequisite shared by A and C. Rendering/Mudclient remain unmeasured. The recommendation
> below (Option D, B-backbone) is unchanged; the immediate next step is the compile gate, then a
> per-opcode net-handler diff.

# RSC Client — Build Options

## Situation

We deobfuscated `rsclassic-1091943135.jar` (RSC rev ~233-235, a Microsoft J++ build) into ~50k lines of readable, named Java: 71 classes plus the assembled `Mudclient.java`, with `docs/NAMING.md` as a verified obf↔readable map for all 71 classes. Critically, **rscplus wraps the *exact same jar*** (`rscplus/assets/rsclassic-1091943135.jar`): it loads each obfuscated class through `JClassLoader`, runs it through `JClassPatcher.patch()` (~5,858 lines of ASM injecting ~433 hook callsites dispatched by obfuscated class name `ua/e/qa/m/client/f/da/lb/sa/wb/pb`), and reads/writes vanilla state via `Reflection.java` (48 fields + 74 methods + 3 constructors, all bound to exact obfuscated names + descriptors). The single most important structural fact that constrains every choice: **the deob is a *comprehension reference, not a compilable build*** — it still imports dead `com.ms.*`/`netscape.javascript` J++ stubs (4 files, all with in-tree pure-Java/AWT fallbacks), and, far worse, `Mudclient.java` (11,559 lines) is only *half-renamed* — it calls the renamed subsystems by their old obfuscated method names (`surface.a(...)` ×322, `world.a(...)` ×30, ~1,535 obf-style call sites total) and declares its own fields/locals with obfuscated types (`ta npc`, `ba surface`, fields `mg/zh/Jh`). There is no build file and no `.class` artifact anywhere under `reference/rsc-client/`, and **zero executable verification of any kind** (no test, no byte-diff, no replay) — so "deob = real client" is today an *unproven* claim resting entirely on human review of a hand-stripped decompile. A second hard constraint: rscplus's hooks match on **bytecode *shape*** (specific `LDC`/`SIPUSH` constants, instruction adjacency, fixed node-walk distances) and its `ClassWriter` uses `COMPUTE_MAXS` only — both of which work *only* because the obf jar's bytecode is frozen, and both of which break the moment you point them at a recompiled deob.

## At-a-glance comparison

| Option | Effort | Risk | G1 (fork/build/run rscplus) | G2 (rscplus features intact) | G3 (replays→cradle) | G4 (render 1:1 audit) | Runnable client from our work? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** — Fork rscplus, re-point patcher + Reflection onto recompiled NAMED deob | VERY HIGH | VERY HIGH | Partial (different target) | At serious risk | Neutral | Indirect | Yes (eventually) |
| **B** — rscplus patches obf jar as-is; deob = reference + bridge only | LOW | LOW | **Yes (full)** | **Yes (full)** | Yes (existing path) | **Yes (textual oracle)** | No (runs the *obf* jar, not our code) |
| **C** — Standalone recompilable pure-Java deob client, no rscplus | HIGH | HIGH | No (abandons rscplus) | No (loses all features) | Neutral | Strong (running oracle) | **Yes (our code)** |
| **D** — Hybrid: compile deob standalone to *prove* it runs, generate remap from NAMING.md, stage the re-point | MED→HIGH (staged) | MED (gated) | Yes (staged) | Protected (staged) | Yes | **Strong** | Yes (staged) |

## Option A — Fork rscplus, re-point `JClassPatcher` + `Reflection` onto recompiled NAMED deob classes

**One-line:** The colleague's stated ideal — keep all of rscplus's machinery but change every hook target from obfuscated names (`ua`, `client`, `Jh`) to our readable names (`client/scene/Surface`, `client/Mudclient`, `clientStream`), loading our recompiled deob instead of the jar.

**How it works:** Rewrite every `node.name.equals("ua")` → `"client/scene/Surface"`; every `hookClassVariable(... "client","Jh","Lda;" ...)` → `(... "client/Mudclient","clientStream","Lclient/net/ClientStream;" ...)`; every emitted `FieldInsnNode/MethodInsnNode/TypeInsnNode` owner+name+desc to its named equivalent; and every exact-descriptor constant in `Reflection.java` (it matches via `method.toGenericString().equals("private final ta client.b(int,byte)")` — exact-string, not structural). Then load the recompiled deob through `JClassLoader`.

**Prerequisites:** (1) the deob must *compile* — i.e. all of Option C's prerequisites first, including the HIGH-effort `Mudclient.java` name reconciliation (~1,535 obf call sites); (2) a complete per-field obf→name table *with descriptors* for `client` (484 fields), `ta`, and `qa` — which **neither `NAMING.md` (class-level only) nor `MUDCLIENT_SKELETON.md` (load-bearing subset only) fully provides today**; (3) resolution of the `m` conflict (rscplus's `patchData` hooks `m.a([BBZ)V`, but `NAMING.md` labels `m`=SocketFactory — Report 5 confirms these are the *same* class/method, just rscplus's *nickname* `patchData` is misleading) and the Scene/World label (`lb`=Scene, `k`=World per NAMING + patcher usage; `MUDCLIENT_SKELETON.md` has them swapped).

**Effort: VERY HIGH.** ~300 string edits across the patcher + Reflection (27 distinct classes, ~166 mirror tuples, 57 field-rewrite tuples, 7 method-rewrite tuples, all Reflection descriptor constants), *plus* the entire Option C build effort as a hard prerequisite, *plus* re-deriving the missing per-field descriptor tables.

**Risk: VERY HIGH**, and the risk is *structural*, not just volume:
- **Bytecode-shape fragility (Report 2's biggest single risk):** the patcher's `find*` loops match on exact constants (`LDC 334.0`, `SIPUSH 510`), instruction adjacency, and `getPrevious().getPrevious()` walks. A modern `javac` recompile of the deob produces *different* bytecode (constant folding, `StringBuilder` vs concat, branch ordering) — **every shape-matched hook breaks even with perfectly correct names.**
- **`COMPUTE_FRAMES`:** the writer uses `COMPUTE_MAXS` only, which works because vanilla J++ classes have no stack-map frames. A recompiled JDK-8+ deob *has* frames, so control-flow-changing transforms produce `VerifyError` unless you switch to `COMPUTE_FRAMES` + a superclass-resolving `ClassWriter` over the named hierarchy.
- **Silent-null descriptor mismatches:** Reflection tolerates `null` (`if (x != null) x.setAccessible(true)`), so a single wrong descriptor among ~230 members *fails silently at runtime*, not at compile.
- **Unverified fidelity (Report 5):** you'd be running ~50k lines of guard-stripped, hand-reconciled code with no executable proof it matches the jar — G2 (replay byte-fidelity) and login (the RSA-key path) are exactly where subtle divergence bites.

**Pros:** Delivers the literal stated goal — rscplus driving *our named code*. Maximally legible end state for future hacking. Forces the deob to a provably-linkable state.

**Cons:** Highest cost and highest risk of any option; inherits *all* of C's compile cost *and* adds the shape-fragility/`COMPUTE_FRAMES` re-derivation on top; the gating artifact (full per-field descriptor map) doesn't exist yet; jeopardizes G2 because every shape-matched hook (ZZZ scaling, right-click bounds, font toggle, combat-menu, replay stream dumps) must be re-derived and re-validated.

**Serves:** G1 (in a transformed sense). **Sacrifices/endangers:** G2 (feature + replay fidelity at serious risk), and pays an enormous tax for benefits that B largely delivers at near-zero risk.

## Option B — Keep rscplus patching the obfuscated jar as-is; deob = comprehension reference + obf↔name bridge

**One-line:** Fork and build rscplus *unchanged* against the existing obf jar; use the deob (and `NAMING.md`) only for *reading* — to author/verify hooks and to drive the G4 render audit.

**How it works:** Nothing about rscplus's runtime changes — it keeps ASM-patching the *frozen, signed, working* obf jar by obf name, so every shape-matched hook and every silent-null reflection lookup keeps working exactly as designed. The deob is consumed as text: `NAMING.md` + the per-member `// obf:` annotations turn obf hook targets into human meaning, and `scene/Surface.java`, `scene/Scene.java`, `world/World.java`, `scene/GameModel.java` serve as the textual ground-truth oracle for the Go `render/` audit (the Go port already cites these files by line number).

**Prerequisites:** Install Ant + a JDK ≤17 (Report 3 verified `BUILD SUCCESSFUL` + a runnable 10 MB `dist/rscplus.jar` under JDK 17; note `java.applet` was removed in JDK 21+, and this box's default `java` is JDK 26, which breaks the build). For *running*: a world `.ini` (URL/port/RSA) in `~/.config/RSCPlus/Worlds/`, or a replay directory for the offline `ReplayServer` loopback path (no live server required).

**Effort: LOW.** rscplus already compiles and packages cleanly; all deps are vendored in `lib/`. The only environment gaps are "install Ant" and "pin a JDK ≤17."

**Risk: LOW.** Names stay cosmetic; bytecode shapes are unchanged; no `COMPUTE_FRAMES` concern; no fidelity question (you're running the real jar). The deob's *unverified-correctness* risk (Report 5) is irrelevant here because the deob never executes.

**Pros:** Immediate working client; full G2 (replay, config, hooks, overlays all intact); zero exposure to the shape-fragility / descriptor-fidelity / J++-stub problems; directly unblocks G4 (the named source is *already* the chosen oracle and needs no compilation). Lowest-risk path to "build and run rscplus."

**Cons:** Does **not** produce "a client from *our* code" — rscplus still runs the obfuscated jar; our deob contributes understanding, not execution. Does not advance G1's literal "re-point at our code" ambition.

**Serves:** G1 (build+run rscplus), G2 (fully), G3 (existing live + replay paths unchanged), G4 (textual oracle, immediately). **Sacrifices:** the "our deob is the running client" aspiration only.

## Option C — Standalone recompilable pure-Java deob client (no rscplus)

**One-line:** Finish the deob into a self-contained, runnable pure-Java/AWT application with its own `main()`, independent of rscplus.

**How it works:** Kill the 4 dead-dep files (`DirectSoundPlayer`, `DirectDrawModes`, `Win32MouseCallback`, `JSBridge`) — each has a working in-tree AWT twin (`SourceLinePlayer`, `DisplayModeSetter`, `RobotCursor`, and a 1-line JSBridge stub); rewire `LoaderThread`'s `Class.forName("ha"/"j"/"rb")` reflective seams to direct typed calls; settle the `GameShell` base (keep `extends Applet` on JDK ≤17, or re-base on `Panel`/`Canvas`); compile the subsystem packages lowest-dependency-first (util → net/data → world/scene → ui/audio); **reconcile `Mudclient.java`** against the renamed subsystems; add a ~10-line `main()` calling the already-present `startApplication(...)` standalone entry plus a `build.xml`/Gradle.

**Prerequisites:** A complete *method-/field-level* obf→name map (the `// obf:` member comments make this extractable but laborious; a full map does not exist in the docs today). JDK ≤17 for the `java.applet` path (or do the `Panel`/`Canvas` re-base).

**Effort: HIGH.** Report 1 is precise: tasks 1–5 (kill dead deps, fix native seam, settle entry point) are ~1–2 days LOW; tasks 6/8 (compile subsystems + launcher) ~1 week MED; but **task 7 — reconciling Mudclient's ~1,535 obf call sites + obf field types — dominates the schedule at multiple weeks**, because Mudclient was deliberately left half-renamed.

**Risk: HIGH** (Report 5): no executable verification exists; ~236 first-pass bugs were *fixed*, which signals how bug-prone the process was, not how many survive. The catastrophic surface is the net layer (one bit wrong in ISAAC/RSA/XTEA/framing → desync or login failure); the perception surface (Scene/World "100% method presence" ≠ behavioral correctness) is G4's exact failure mode. The deob's baked-in modulus is the hex `ca950472…`, but rscplus *overrides* it per-world (`JConfig.SERVER_RSA_MODULUS`, decimal) — a standalone client must reproduce a key-override mechanism or fail to authenticate against real worlds.

**Pros:** The *only* option whose running artifact is genuinely *our code*. A running deob is the gold-standard diff oracle for G4 (render the same scene in both deob and jar, pixel-diff) and the strongest fidelity proof for the protocol. Forces the deob to a provably-linkable, testable state.

**Cons:** Highest non-staged risk; abandons all of rscplus's mature value — replay capture/playback, config, overlays, the `ReplayServer` loopback (G2 and the G3 substrate all lost); the Mudclient reconciliation is a multi-week slog with no intermediate payoff until it links.

**Serves:** the "runnable client from our work" goal, and G4 (as a running oracle). **Sacrifices:** G1's *rscplus* framing, G2 entirely, and the G3 replay substrate.

## Option D — Hybrid / staged (recommended shape)

**One-line:** Treat the deob's compilability and fidelity as *experiments to run cheaply*, mechanize the re-point mapping instead of hand-editing it, and only commit to the expensive A/C steps after the cheap gates pass — all while B keeps a working client in hand the entire time.

**How it works — the concrete staged plan:**
1. **Stage 0 (B as the safety net):** Fork + build rscplus unchanged against the obf jar (Report 3: known-good). You have a running, full-featured client and the G4 textual oracle on day one. Nothing below can regress this.
2. **Stage 1 — cheapest experiments that most reduce uncertainty (Report 5 Tiers 0–1):**
   - **Constant audit (hours, no build):** `javap -c -p` the jar and assert every decoded constant in the deob matches — RSA modulus, ISAAC/XTEA shift reductions (13/6/2/16, …), the `0x9E3779B9` delta, framing thresholds (160/128/32768), XOR string-pool keys (self-checking via the deob's own decoders). This catches the whole "decoded constants are claims" class immediately.
   - **"Does the deob even compile?":** Execute Report 1's LOW tasks 1–5 (delete the 4 J++ files, stub `JSBridge`, rewire `LoaderThread`, settle `GameShell` base) and `javac` the subsystem packages lowest-dependency-first. *Failure to compile is itself a fidelity signal* (missing/renamed members). This is the single cheapest experiment that most reduces uncertainty about A and C.
3. **Stage 2 — mechanize the bridge (de-risks A's ~300 hand-edits):** Derive a complete per-member obf→name+descriptor table by extracting the `// obf:` annotations from the 71 deob files, and **generate** the `JClassPatcher`/`Reflection` remap from `NAMING.md` + that table rather than editing ~300 strings by hand. While generating, resolve the `m` nickname and the Scene/World label discrepancy as data, not prose.
4. **Stage 3 — primitive equivalence tests (Report 5 Tier 3):** Use rscplus's own `JClassLoader` to load the real obf classes and assert the *compiled deob* matches them on fixed inputs — ISAAC keystream from a seed, every `Buffer`/`BitBuffer` round-trip, XTEA of a known block, `World` wall/route over a tile battery (this last one *also* directly validates the Go `render/` port). This pins the protocol + collision primitives before any re-point.
5. **Stage 4 — gated re-point:** Only now attempt Option A's re-point, having compiled the deob (Stage 1), generated the mapping (Stage 2), and proven the primitives (Stage 3). Budget explicitly for `COMPUTE_FRAMES` + re-deriving the shape-matched hooks. If Stage 4 proves too fragile, you *still* have B running and have gained a tested standalone deob (C) as a render oracle.

**Prerequisites:** Same toolchain as B (Ant + JDK ≤17). Each stage gates the next.

**Effort: MEDIUM, staged** — Stages 0–1 are days; Stages 2–3 are the bounded mechanizable middle; Stage 4 is the only HIGH chunk, and you reach it already de-risked (or you stop short with real value banked).

**Risk: MEDIUM and *gated*** — the defining feature is that risk is *bought down incrementally* and B is never at risk. The expensive, fragile A-work is attempted only after the cheap checks have either built confidence or killed the idea early.

**Pros:** Keeps a working, full-feature client (G2) throughout; answers "does it compile?" and "is it 1:1?" *before* paying for the re-point; mechanizes the ~300-edit mapping (turning A's most error-prone step into generated, auditable data); the Stage-3 `World` route tests *simultaneously* validate the Go render lib (G4); every stage produces standalone value even if you stop.

**Cons:** More coordination/process than a single push; no single dramatic deliverable; if the ultimate aim really is "rscplus on our code ASAP," D's discipline feels slower than diving straight at A (but A-direct is the higher-expected-cost path given the shape-fragility findings).

**Serves:** all four goals, sequenced — G1/G2 immediately via Stage 0, G4 via Stage 0 textual + Stage 3 route tests, G3 unaffected, and G1's *literal* re-point reached at Stage 4 only if the gates justify it.

## Recommendation

**Adopt Option D (staged hybrid), with Option B as its permanent Stage 0 backbone.** B alone fully satisfies G1's *practical* meaning ("be able to BUILD and RUN rscplus"), all of G2, the G3 substrate, and G4's textual audit — at LOW effort and LOW risk — so there is no reason to ever *not* have B running. Option A (the colleague's literal ideal) is real but is the *highest-cost, highest-risk* path, and crucially its worst risks (bytecode-shape fragility, `COMPUTE_FRAMES`, silent descriptor mismatches, unverified fidelity) cannot be retired by effort alone — they must be *measured*. D measures them cheaply first and mechanizes A's most error-prone step, so if A is viable, D is the safest road to it, and if A is not viable, D fails cheaply with B still in hand.

**Week-1 milestone (the cheapest experiment that most reduces uncertainty):**
1. **Day 1:** Stand up B — install Ant + JDK 17, run `ant clean dist`, confirm the 10 MB `dist/rscplus.jar` runs (against a replay directory, no live server needed). *Working client banked.*
2. **Days 1–2:** Run the **constant audit** (Report 5 Tier 0) — `javap -c -p` the jar, assert the deob's RSA modulus, ISAAC/XTEA shifts/delta, framing thresholds, and XOR keys. Pure reading, no build, immediately flags any decoded-constant divergence and the RSA-key-override issue.
3. **Days 2–5:** **Answer "does the deob even compile?"** — execute Report 1's LOW tasks 1–5 (delete `DirectSoundPlayer`/`DirectDrawModes`/`Win32MouseCallback`, stub `JSBridge`, rewire `LoaderThread`, settle `GameShell`), then `javac` the subsystem packages in dependency order (`util` → `net`/`data` → `world`/`scene`). **Stop at `Mudclient.java`** — do *not* yet pay the multi-week reconciliation. The deliverable is a yes/no on "do the *subsystems* compile cleanly," which is the load-bearing precondition for both A and C and tells us whether the deob's named layer is link-consistent at all.

If the subsystems compile and the constants match, proceed to Stage 2/3 (mechanize the mapping, run primitive equivalence tests via `JClassLoader`) before committing to the Stage-4 re-point. If they don't, we've spent <1 week to learn the re-point is premature — and B is still serving every practical goal.

## Open questions / things to validate

- **The `m` conflict:** Confirm by disassembling `m.a([BBZ)V` from the jar that rscplus's `patchData` target and `NAMING.md`'s `m`=SocketFactory are the *same* class/method (Report 5 says yes — `SocketFactory.initGameData` — but it must be settled as data before any re-point binds to it). Bind re-point hooks strictly by **obf name + descriptor**, never by rscplus's misleading nicknames (`patchData`, `patchRandom`, `patchGameApplet`).
- **Scene/World label:** `NAMING.md` (lines 18-19) prose still says the swap "needs to be done," contradicting the corrected code and `INDEX.md`; `MUDCLIENT_SKELETON.md` still has `Hh=scene→k`, `Ek=world→lb` *swapped*. Fix both docs so no one "re-swaps" already-correct files.
- **Missing per-field descriptor map:** A complete obf→name+descriptor table for `client` (484 fields), `ta`, and `qa` is the gating artifact for A/C and does not exist today (NAMING is class-level; SKELETON is a subset). Validate it is extractable from the `// obf:` member annotations.
- **RSA key override:** Any deob-as-client path must reproduce rscplus's runtime modulus override (`JConfig.SERVER_RSA_MODULUS`, a *decimal* per-world key) instead of the jar's baked-in hex `ca950472…`, or login fails against rscplus's target worlds.
- **Go render port line citations:** The Go `render/` library cites `World.java`/`Scene.java` by line number; re-validate these against the *current* files post World/Scene-swap (commit `960310c`) to ensure they didn't pin to pre-swap lines.
- **`java.applet` ceiling:** This box's default `java` is JDK 26 (no `java.applet`); only JDK 17 and 26 are installed (no 8/11). Decide: pin JDK ≤17 (install one), or do the `Panel`/`Canvas` re-base. Ant is also not installed (only a non-executable bundled copy under `openrsc`).
- **Replay→cradle is unbuilt (G3):** The cradle is a *standalone Go client* that perceives via its own `proto/v235`-decoded `world.World` and never touches rscplus or the jar. rscplus replays are ISAAC-encrypted Java-side packet logs that only feed the *vanilla Java client* over a loopback socket. If captured-replay→cradle is wanted, it requires a *new* Go replay reader on `proto/v235` (decode `keys.bin`→ISAAC, feed `in.bin.gz` through `inbound.go` into `world.World.Apply`) — do **not** try to pipe the cradle through rscplus's `ReplayServer`. Confirm whether deterministic *live* `.routine` scenarios already suffice for G3 (Report 4 says they do).
- **Headless launch for drones:** rscplus has *no* `args`/CLI parsing and *no* headless support (`Launcher extends JFrame`, unconditional `setVisible(true)`, no `java.awt.headless`). Driving many drones through rscplus would require adding a scripted entry point + Xvfb — but per Report 4 the cradle doesn't need rscplus for G3 at all, so validate whether this is even in scope.
- **Residual fidelity after Tiers 0–3:** Even if constants match and primitives pass, full G3/G4 trust needs Tier 4 (replay/traffic byte-diff: deob vs jar through the same replay) and Tier 5 (three-way render reconciliation: Go `render/` vs deob vs jar on identical scene state). Treat these as the bar for "1:1," not the cheaper early tiers.
